---
title: "The Evolution of Geographical Organizations in India: A Historical Perspective"
date: "2026-02-19"
categories: [History, Geography, Institutional Growth, India]
image: "social/institutions_list.png"
description: "Tracing the journey of geographical societies in India from the early 20th century to the modern geospatial era."
---

Geography as an academic discipline in India has seen a remarkable evolution, transitioning from a colonial descriptive tool to a high-tech spatial science. Central to this journey has been the birth and growth of various geographical organizations and societies. These bodies have not only provided a platform for researchers but have also shaped the curriculum and policy advocacy for the discipline [1][2].

## The Early Foundations (1920s - 1940s)

The formal institutionalization of Geography in India began in the early 1920s. The **[Indian Geographical Society (IGS)](http://www.igschennai.org/)**, established in **1926** in Chennai (as the Madras Geographical Association), holds the distinction of being the oldest [3][4]. Founded by **Prof. N. Subrahmanyam**, it paved the way for professional geographical discourse [5].

Soon after, the **[Geographical Society of India (GSI)](http://geographicalsocietyofindia.org.in/)** was founded on July 29, **1933** in Kolkata (initially as the Calcutta Geographical Society) by a group of thirteen founding members [8][9]. Following independence, the **[National Geographical Society of India (NGSI)](http://ngsi.in/)** was established in **1946** at Banaras Hindu University (BHU), Varanasi, by **Prof. Harbans Lal Chhibbar**, marking a new chapter in post-colonial geographical research [13][14].

## The Growth of National & Specialized Bodies (1960s - 1980s)

As the discipline matured, the need for a national umbrella organization led to the establishment of the **[National Association of Geographers, India (NAGI)](http://nagi.org.in/)** in **1978** [10][11]. Based at the Delhi School of Economics, NAGI has since been the primary coordinator for the annual Indian Geography Congress [12].

This period also saw the rise of specialized societies:
*   **Indian Society of Remote Sensing (ISRS)** (1969): Bridging geography with space technology [1].
*   **[Association of Population Geographers of India](http://apgi.in/)** (1978): Focusing on the demographic complexities of India [16].
*   **[Institute of Indian Geographers (IIG)](http://iigpune.in/)**, Pune (1979): Promoting rigorous scientific research across all sub-fields [7].

## Directory of Geographical Institutions in India (Verified 2024-25)

The table below lists major geographical associations, their current representatives (verified for the 2024-25 term where available), and their historical host institutions.

| Name of the Association/Society | Estd. | Host Institution | Current Representative(s) |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---|
| **[NAGI (National Association)](http://nagi.org.in/)** | 1978 | Delhi School of Economics | **Prof. Salahuddin Qureshi** (President), **Prof. A K Saha** (Secy. General) |
| **[IGS (Chennai)](http://www.igschennai.org/)** | 1926 | University of Madras | Prof. N. Sivagnanam (President), Prof. R. Jaganathan (Gen Secy) |
| **[GSI (Kolkata)](http://geographicalsocietyofindia.org.in/)** | 1933 | University of Calcutta | Smt. Suprova Ray (President), Prof. Apurba Rabi Ghosh (Hony. Secy) |
| **[NGSI (Varanasi)](http://ngsi.in/)** | 1946 | BHU, Varanasi | **Professor V. N. Sharma** (Hony. Secretary) |
| **[IIG (Pune)](http://iigpune.in/)** | 1979 | Pune University | **Prof. D. K. Nayak** (President), **Prof. Ravindra G. Jaybhaye** (Secy General) |
| **[APGI (Chandigarh)](http://apgi.in/)** | 1978 | Panjab University | **Prof. Krishna Mohan** (President), **Dr. Gaurav Kalotra** (Secretary) |
| **[ILEE (Kolkata)](http://ilee.org.in/)** | 1983 | Kolkata | Prof. L. N. Satpati |
| **[IGI (Geomorphologists)](http://www.indiageomorph.org/)** | 1988 | University of Allahabad | Prof. Sunil Kumar De (President) |
| **[Deccan Geographical Society](http://www.dgsi.org.in/)** | 1962 | SP Pune University | Prof. B. C. Vaidya |
| **[NEIGS (Guwahati)](http://neigs.org.in/)** | 1968 | Gauhati University | Prof. Abani K. Bhagabati & Prof. Bimal K. Kar |
| **[MBP (Maharashtra)](http://mbpune.org/)** | 1984 | Pune | Prof. Praveen Saptarshi |
| **[ISG (Geomatics)](http://isgindia.org/)** | 1993 | ISRO-SAC, Ahmedabad | - |
| **[Aligarh Geographical Society](https://www.amu.ac.in/department/geography)** | 1924* | AMU, Aligarh | Prof. Ateeque Ahmed |
| **[Allahabad Geog. Society](https://allduniv.ac.in/department-of-geography)** | - | Allahabad University | Prof. Manorama Sinha |
| **[AGBJ (Bihar/JH)](http://agbj.org.in/)** | - | Patna University | Prof. Devender Prasad Singh |
| **[UGIT (Bengaluru)](http://ugit.net.in/)** | 2000s | Bangalore University | Prof. Ashok Hanjagi |
| **[Uttar Bharat Bhoogol Parishad](http://ubbp.org/)** | - | Gorakhpur | Prof. V. K. Srivatsava |
| **[RGA (Rajasthan)](http://rga.ac.in/)** | - | Bhilwara | Dr. S. S. Bhat |
| **[GAG (Goa)](http://geographersassociationgoa.blogspot.com/)** | - | Goa | Prof. Prabirkumar Rath |

*\*Years marked with asterisks denote early informal society formations or departmental origins.*

## Modern Era: Geospatial Expansion & GIS

In the 21st century, organizations like the **Association of Geospatial Industries (AGI)** and **Indian Society of Geomatics (ISG)** have taken the lead in integrating satellite-based remote sensing, LiDAR, and GIS into the geographical framework [4][5]. These organizations foster collaboration between the industry and academia, ensuring that Indian geographers are at the forefront of the global spatial revolution [3].

The recent participation of Indian representatives in the **Asian Geographical Association (founded 2018)**, where Prof. Suresh Chand Rai represented NAGI, further exemplifies the global reach of Indian geographical research [12][13].

## 📅 Upcoming Geographical Events (2025-2026)

Staying connected with the academic community is vital. Here are the key upcoming conferences and meets organized by major Indian geographical bodies:

*   **9th NAGI International Conference 2025**
    *   *Theme:* Sustainable Future Earth: Emerging Issues and Challenges
    *   *Dates:* October 7-9, 2025
    *   *Host:* Ranchi University, Ranchi
*   **46th IIG Annual Meet & International Conference 2025**
    *   *Theme:* Dynamic Earth, Fragile Environment, and Climate-Resilient Societies
    *   *Dates:* October 25-27, 2025
    *   *Host:* Hemvati Nandan Bahuguna Garhwal University (HNBGU), Srinagar, Uttarakhand
*   **47th Indian Geography Congress (NAGI) 2025**
    *   *Theme:* Geo Vision for Viksit Bharat @ 2047
    *   *Dates:* November 13-15, 2025
    *   *Host:* University of Rajasthan, Jaipur
*   **ISG-ISRS National Symposium 2025**
    *   *Focus:* Advancements in Geomatics and Remote Sensing
    *   *Dates:* November 25-27, 2025
*   **3rd Indian Quaternary Congress 2026**
    *   *Dates:* January 19-23, 2026

## ✅ Recently Concluded Events (2024)

The past year was highly active for the geographical community in India, with several landmark meets:

*   **46th Indian Geography Congress (NAGI):** Organized by Osmania University, Hyderabad (Dec 27-29, 2024).
*   **45th IIG Annual Meet:** Hosted by North-Eastern Hill University (NEHU), Shillong (Dec 19-21, 2024).
*   **Geo India 2024:** 6th South Asian Geosciences Conference at Greater Noida (Nov 15-17, 2024).
*   **44th IIG Annual Meet:** Held at Cotton University, Guwahati (Jan 22-24, 2024).

## Conclusion

From the local "Madras Geographical Association" of 1926 to the high-tech geospatial societies of today, geographical organizations in India have been the pillars of the discipline. They continue to play a crucial role in addressing national challenges—from urban planning and disaster management to climate change analysis—demonstrating that Geography is not just about mapping the world, but about understanding and improving it.

## References

1. Indian Society of Remote Sensing (ISRS). "About ISRS: History and Objectives." Available at [isrsindia.in](https://isrsindia.in).
2. National Association of Geographers, India (NAGI). "Official Lists: Executive Committee 2024-25." [nagi.org.in](http://nagi.org.in).
3. Indian Geographical Society (IGS). "History of IGS Chennai." *Madras Geographical Journal* archival records.
4. University of Madras. "Department of Geography: Research & Societies." [unom.ac.in](https://unom.ac.in).
5. Foundation of Practising Geographer. "Evolution of Geographical Discourse in India." [practicing-geographer.org](http://practicing-geographer.org).
6. IIG Pune. "Constitution and Executive Council 2024-25." [iigeo.org](https://iigeo.org).
7. Wikipedia. "National Geographical Society of India." [en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Geographical_Society_of_India](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Geographical_Society_of_India).
8. Geographical Society of India. "Founding Principles and History." [geographicalsocietyofindia.org.in](http://geographicalsocietyofindia.org.in).
9. Association of Population Geographers of India (APGI). "Executive Body Term 2023-2025." [apgin.org](http://apgin.org).
10. Shodhganga. "Institutional Framework for Geography in India." [inflibnet.ac.in](http://inflibnet.ac.in).
11. AMU Department of Geography. "Our Heritage: The Aligarh Geographical Society." [amu.ac.in](http://amu.ac.in).
12. Asian Geographical Association (AGA). "Founding Committee and Member Countries." [asiageo.net](http://asiageo.net).
13. Geo-DOI Archive. "Report on the Formation of Asian Geographical Association in 2018." [geodoi.ac.in](http://geodoi.ac.in).
14. National Geographical Society of India (NGSI). "Publications and Secretary Journals." [ngsi.in](http://ngsi.in).
